expanded_description:
Money demand is not constant; it varies systematically with economic conditions. Understanding the factors that shift money demand is essential for central banks because changes in money demand, if unaccounted for, can lead to policy errors. If the central bank targets a specific interest rate, an increase in money demand will raise rates (tightening policy) unless the central bank accommodates by increasing money supply. If the central bank targets money supply, an increase in money demand will lower velocity and may cause deflation. Thus, central banks must forecast changes in money demand.

The three primary factors affecting money demand are income (real GDP), the price level, and interest rates. Income affects money demand because higher economic activity requires more transactions, increasing the demand for money. The price level affects money demand because people need more nominal money to buy the same real goods when prices rise. Interest rates affect money demand through the opportunity cost: higher rates make holding money more expensive, reducing demand. Additional factors include financial innovation (ATMs, credit cards, mobile payments), uncertainty (recessions, crises increase precautionary demand), and institutional factors (reserve requirements, payment system efficiency).

In this section, we will examine how each factor shifts the money demand curve, distinguish between movements along the curve (interest rate changes) and shifts of the curve (income, price level, technology), analyze the empirical stability of money demand, and explore the implications for monetary policy. You will learn to calculate income elasticity and interest elasticity, interpret money demand regressions, understand why money demand became unstable in the 1980s (the 'missing money' puzzle), and evaluate how financial innovation has permanently reduced money demand. Mastering these factors is essential for forecasting money demand, designing policy rules, and understanding the limits of monetary targeting.

core_concept.principle:
Money demand is determined by three primary factors and several secondary factors:

1. Income (Real GDP, Y): Higher income → more transactions → higher money demand. Money demand increases proportionally or less than proportionally with income. The income elasticity of money demand (percentage change in money demand for a 1% change in income) is typically estimated between 0.5 and 1.5. If elasticity = 1, money demand is proportional to income (constant velocity). If elasticity < 1, velocity rises with income.

2. Price Level (P): Money demand is demand for real money balances ($M_d/P$). A doubling of the price level doubles nominal money demand, leaving real money demand unchanged. In the long run, money demand is homogeneous of degree 1 in prices (no money illusion). In the short run, money illusion may exist (people adjust slowly).

3. Interest Rate (i): Higher interest rates increase the opportunity cost of holding money, reducing money demand. The interest elasticity (percentage change in money demand for a 1% change in i) is typically estimated between -0.1 and -0.5 in the short run, and higher in the long run.

Real Money Demand Function:
$\frac{M_d}{P} = L(Y, i, Z)$
where $Z$ captures other factors (financial innovation, uncertainty, payment technology).

Secondary Factors:
• Financial innovation: ATMs, credit cards, debit cards, mobile payments reduce money demand.
• Uncertainty: Recessions, crises, pandemics increase precautionary money demand.
• Inflation expectations: Expected inflation reduces money demand (higher opportunity cost).
• Wealth: Higher wealth increases money demand (portfolio motive).
• Payment timing: Frequency of income receipt affects average cash balances.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine your income doubles from \$50,000 to \$100,000. You will likely hold more money in your checking account to cover higher spending—perhaps not double, but significantly more. This is the income effect. Now imagine the price level doubles (inflation). You will need twice as much nominal money to buy the same goods, so your nominal money demand doubles, but your real money demand (purchasing power) stays the same. This is the price level effect, assuming no money illusion.

Now suppose the interest rate on savings accounts rises from 1% to 5%. The opportunity cost of keeping money in your checking account (earning 0%) rises. You will transfer some funds to savings, reducing your checking balance. This is the interest rate effect. The size of this effect depends on how sensitive you are to interest rates—the interest elasticity.

Finally, consider the impact of mobile banking apps. In the 1980s, converting savings to checking required a trip to the bank. Today, it takes 10 seconds on your phone. This financial innovation reduces the cost of holding low-interest checking balances, so you might hold less in checking and more in savings. Money demand falls. Central banks must account for such structural changes when forecasting money demand, or they will make policy errors—as the Fed did in the 1980s when the 'missing money' puzzle appeared.

image.alt: Diagram: Three arrows pointing to money demand curve: Income (up arrow, increasing demand), Price level (up arrow, increasing demand), Interest rate (down arrow, decreasing demand). Secondary factors listed below.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.1: Factors affecting money demand: income and price level shift demand outward; interest rates cause movements along the curve.

sections.introduction:
Money demand is not constant; it varies systematically with economic conditions. Understanding the factors that shift money demand is essential for central banks because changes in money demand, if unaccounted for, can lead to policy errors. If the central bank targets a specific interest rate, an increase in money demand will raise rates (tightening policy) unless the central bank accommodates by increasing money supply. If the central bank targets money supply, an increase in money demand will lower velocity and may cause deflation. Thus, central banks must forecast changes in money demand.

The three primary factors affecting money demand are income (real GDP), the price level, and interest rates. Income affects money demand because higher economic activity requires more transactions. The price level affects money demand because people need more nominal money to buy the same real goods when prices rise. Interest rates affect money demand through the opportunity cost: higher rates make holding money more expensive, reducing demand. Additional factors include financial innovation (ATMs, mobile payments), uncertainty (crises increase precautionary demand), and institutional factors (reserve requirements, payment system efficiency).

In this section, we will examine how each factor shifts the money demand curve, distinguish between movements along the curve (interest rate changes) and shifts of the curve (income, price level, technology), analyze the empirical stability of money demand, and explore the implications for monetary policy. You will learn to calculate income elasticity and interest elasticity, interpret money demand regressions, understand why money demand became unstable in the 1980s (the 'missing money' puzzle), and evaluate how financial innovation has permanently reduced money demand.

introduction_image.alt: Graph: Money demand curve shifting right due to higher income or higher price level; movement along curve due to interest rate change; secondary shifts shown as dashed curves.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.3.2: Movements along vs. shifts of the money demand curve: interest rates cause movements; income, price level, and technology cause shifts.

key_concept.definition:
The money demand function relates real money balances ($M/P$) to its determinants:

$\frac{M_d}{P} = L(Y, i, Z)$

where:
• $Y$ = real income (GDP)
• $i$ = nominal interest rate (opportunity cost)
• $Z$ = other factors (financial innovation, uncertainty, inflation expectations, wealth, payment timing)

Income Effect: Higher income increases money demand. The income elasticity is $\eta_Y = \frac{\partial M/M}{\partial Y/Y}$. Empirical estimates range from 0.5 to 1.5. If $\eta_Y = 1$, velocity is constant (quantity theory). If $\eta_Y < 1$, velocity rises with income (financial deepening reduces money demand as share of income).

Price Level Effect: In the long run, a 1% increase in prices leads to a 1% increase in nominal money demand (no money illusion). Real money demand is unchanged. In the short run, adjustment may be incomplete due to sticky expectations or adjustment costs.

Interest Rate Effect: Higher interest rates reduce money demand. The interest semi-elasticity (percentage change in money demand for a 1 percentage point change in i) is typically -0.05 to -0.10 (i.e., a 1% point rate increase reduces money demand by 5-10%). The interest elasticity (percentage change for a 1% change in i) is smaller because interest rates are already small numbers.

Secondary Factors:
• Financial innovation: Reduces money demand (ATMs, credit cards, mobile payments, FedNow).
• Uncertainty: Increases precautionary money demand (recessions, crises, pandemics).
• Inflation expectations: Higher expected inflation reduces money demand (opportunity cost).
• Wealth: Higher wealth increases money demand (portfolio allocation).
• Payment timing: More frequent income payments reduce average money demand.
key_concept.context: The empirical study of money demand dates back to the 1950s-1960s, when researchers estimated stable money demand functions for the US and other countries. The 1970s saw the breakdown of these relationships due to financial innovation (NOW accounts, money market funds). The 'missing money' puzzle (Goldfeld, 1976) described the persistent overprediction of money demand—actual money demand was lower than models predicted. In the 1980s-1990s, researchers added financial innovation proxies (e.g., number of ATMs, credit card usage) to restore stability. Today, money demand is understood to be subject to structural breaks, requiring central banks to use judgment alongside econometric models. The 2008 crisis and 2020 pandemic caused sharp increases in precautionary money demand (velocity collapse), demonstrating the importance of uncertainty as a factor.

image.alt: 3D surface: Money demand on z-axis, income on x-axis, interest rate on y-axis; surface slopes up with income, down with interest rate.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.3: The money demand surface: $M_d/P = L(Y, i)$; income increases demand; interest rates decrease demand.

classifications.type: Income and the Income Elasticity of Money Demand
classifications.value: Higher income (real GDP) increases money demand; the elasticity determines whether velocity is constant, rising, or falling
characteristics: Income elasticity ($\eta_Y$) measures % change in money demand per 1% change in income
$\eta_Y = 1$: money demand proportional to income (constant velocity)
$\eta_Y > 1$: money demand grows faster than income (velocity falls as economy grows)
$\eta_Y < 1$: money demand grows slower than income (velocity rises with income)
Empirical estimates: short-run 0.5-1.0; long-run 1.0-1.5
classifications.behavior:
The relationship between income and money demand is fundamental to the quantity theory. If $\eta_Y = 1$, then velocity ($V = PY/M$) is constant. If $\eta_Y < 1$, velocity rises with income (people economize on cash balances as they get richer). If $\eta_Y > 1$, velocity falls (money is a luxury good; wealthier people hold proportionally more cash).

Empirical evidence suggests $\eta_Y$ is close to 1 in the long run but less than 1 in the short run. This means that during economic booms, velocity tends to rise (money demand does not keep up with income). During recessions, velocity falls (money demand is stickier downward). This procyclical velocity pattern amplifies business cycles: in a boom, velocity rises, increasing nominal spending; in a bust, velocity falls, reducing spending.

The income elasticity also varies across countries. In developing economies with less financial sophistication, $\eta_Y$ tends to be higher (closer to 1.5) because cash is the primary transaction medium. In advanced economies with credit cards and mobile payments, $\eta_Y$ is lower (0.5-0.8). Financial innovation has reduced the income elasticity over time.

image.alt: Graph: Money demand vs. income on log scales; three lines: slope 1 (unit elasticity), slope >1 (elastic), slope <1 (inelastic).
image.caption: Figure 7.3.4: Income elasticity of money demand: slope of log(M) vs. log(Y); unit elasticity means constant velocity.

examples.explanation: Year 1: Real GDP = \$10T, Real M2 = \$5T. Year 2: Real GDP = \$11T (10% growth), Real M2 = \$5.4T (8% growth). Income elasticity = 8%/10% = 0.8. Velocity rose because money demand grew slower than income.

In the US (high credit card usage), estimated $\eta_Y$ for M1 is 0.5-0.7. In China (cash-heavy economy), $\eta_Y$ for M1 is 1.2-1.5. As China's payment system modernizes, its income elasticity is falling toward US levels.

classifications.type: Interest Rate and Interest Elasticity of Money Demand
classifications.value: Higher interest rates increase the opportunity cost of holding money, reducing money demand
characteristics: Interest semi-elasticity: % change in M for a 1 percentage point change in i (e.g., 5% to 6%)
Typical semi-elasticity: -0.05 to -0.10 (a 1% point rate rise reduces M by 5-10%)
Interest elasticity: % change in M for a 1% change in i (much smaller because i is small)
Higher elasticity at low interest rates (liquidity trap: infinite at zero)
Long-run elasticity higher than short-run (adjustment takes time)
classifications.behavior:
The interest rate is the opportunity cost of holding non-interest-bearing money. When rates rise, households and firms reduce their money balances by shifting funds to interest-bearing accounts (savings, money market funds) or by reducing cash holdings. The magnitude of this response is the interest elasticity.

At very low interest rates (near zero), the opportunity cost is minimal, so money demand is high and insensitive to small rate changes. At very high rates, money demand is low and may become more elastic. The liquidity trap represents the extreme case: at zero rates, money demand is perfectly elastic (horizontal).

Financial innovation has increased interest elasticity over time because it has reduced transaction costs. In the 1950s, moving money from savings to checking required a trip to the bank (high cost), so interest elasticity was low. Today, mobile transfers are instantaneous and free, so interest elasticity is higher. This means that modern central banks must be more cautious about interest rate changes—they have larger effects on money demand and velocity.

image.alt: Graph: Money demand curve steep at high interest rates (inelastic), flat at low interest rates (elastic), horizontal at zero (perfectly elastic).
image.caption: Figure 7.3.5: Interest elasticity varies with the level of interest rates: low rates produce high elasticity (liquidity trap); high rates produce low elasticity.

examples.explanation: Interest rate rises from 2% to 3% (1 percentage point increase). Money demand falls from \$1,000B to \$950B (5% decrease). Semi-elasticity = -5% / 1% point = -5.0. This means each 1% point rate increase reduces money demand by 5%.

examples.process: Zero Lower Bound and Liquidity Trap
examples.explanation: In 2009, the Fed cut rates to 0.25%. Money demand became highly elastic. The Fed injected \$1T in reserves via QE, but M2 grew only modestly—people held the extra reserves as idle balances because the opportunity cost of holding money was near zero. The interest semi-elasticity approached infinity (perfectly elastic demand).

classifications.type: Price Level: Money Illusion vs. Long-Run Neutrality
classifications.value: In the long run, money demand is proportional to the price level (no money illusion); in the short run, adjustment may be incomplete
characteristics: Long-run price elasticity = 1 (homogeneity of degree 1 in prices)
Short-run price elasticity may be <1 due to sticky expectations or adjustment costs
Money illusion: temporary confusion between nominal and real values
Inflation expectations affect money demand even before prices change
Hyperinflation: money demand collapses as people avoid holding currency
classifications.behavior:
In the long run, people care about real purchasing power, not nominal dollars. If the price level doubles, nominal money demand doubles, leaving real money demand unchanged. This is called homogeneity of degree 1 in prices or absence of money illusion. Most economic models assume this property.

In the short run, however, money demand may not adjust instantly. If prices rise unexpectedly, people may temporarily hold less real money than desired (money illusion) because they do not immediately adjust their nominal balances. Over time, they withdraw more cash or transfer funds to checking, restoring real balances.

In hyperinflations (Germany 1923, Zimbabwe 2008, Venezuela 2010s), money demand collapses because people refuse to hold rapidly depreciating currency. They convert to foreign currency (dollarization), real assets (real estate, gold), or spend income immediately. The price elasticity becomes negative (higher prices reduce real money demand) because expected inflation dominates the adjustment.

image.alt: Graph: Long-run: nominal money demand proportional to price level (45-degree line). Short-run: lagged adjustment; overshooting in hyperinflation.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.6: Price level elasticity: long-run elasticity = 1 (no money illusion); short-run elasticity may be less than 1 due to adjustment lags.

examples.process: Long-Run Neutrality: Doubling the Money Supply
examples.explanation: If the central bank doubles the money supply, in the long run, prices double, real money supply unchanged, real money demand unchanged. No real effects (monetary neutrality). In the short run, prices may adjust slowly, leading to temporary real effects (monetary non-neutrality).

In Weimar Germany (1923), prices doubled every 2-3 days. People stopped holding marks altogether—they were paid multiple times per day and spent immediately. Real money demand collapsed to near zero. The government had to introduce a new currency (Rentenmark) to restore confidence.

classifications.type: Secondary Factors: Financial Innovation, Uncertainty, and Wealth
classifications.value: Structural factors that shift money demand over time and across episodes
characteristics: Financial innovation (ATMs, credit cards, mobile payments, FedNow): reduces money demand
Uncertainty (recessions, crises, pandemics): increases precautionary money demand
Wealth: higher wealth increases money demand (portfolio motive)
Inflation expectations: higher expected inflation reduces money demand
Payment timing: more frequent income receipt reduces average money demand
Reserve requirements: higher requirements increase bank demand for reserves
classifications.behavior:
These secondary factors explain the instability of money demand over time and the failure of simple models to predict money demand during structural breaks.

Financial innovation has been the most important source of money demand instability since the 1970s. The introduction of NOW accounts (interest-bearing checking) in the 1980s blurred the M1-M2 distinction. Money market funds in the 1990s offered check-writing privileges with higher yields. ATMs and debit cards in the 1990s-2000s reduced the need for cash. Mobile payments (Apple Pay, Google Pay, Venmo) and instant payment systems (FedNow, 2023) further reduce money demand. Each innovation creates a structural break in money demand time series.

Uncertainty increases precautionary money demand. During the 2008 financial crisis, M2 velocity fell from 1.9 to 1.5 as households hoarded cash. During the 2020 pandemic, velocity fell further to 1.1. In both episodes, the increase in money demand was not fully anticipated by models, leading to overpredictions of inflation.

Wealth affects money demand through the portfolio motive. Wealthier individuals hold more money as a store of value, not just for transactions. This is why money demand tends to rise faster than income in very high-income countries (luxury good).

Inflation expectations matter because money loses value when prices rise. If people expect 10% inflation, they will hold less money (spend it quickly or convert to inflation-hedged assets). This is the 'hot potato' effect: the central bank creates money, but people try to pass it off, driving up prices.

image.alt: Timeline: 1970s (NOW accounts), 1980s (ATMs), 1990s (debit cards, online banking), 2000s (mobile payments), 2020s (FedNow); each labeled with effect on money demand (down arrow).
image.caption: Figure 7.3.7: Financial innovation timeline: each innovation has reduced money demand, causing structural breaks in estimated money demand functions.

examples.explanation: In the mid-1970s, econometric models overpredicted money demand—actual money balances were lower than predicted. The cause was financial innovation: the introduction of NOW accounts (interest-bearing checking) and money market funds allowed households to earn interest on near-money, reducing demand for M1. The puzzle was resolved by adding financial innovation proxies to money demand equations.

In March-April 2020, M2 grew at 25% annualized as households received stimulus checks and reduced spending due to lockdowns. Velocity fell from 1.4 to 1.1. Standard money demand models (based on income and interest rates) underpredicted money demand by over \$1 trillion. The missing factor was uncertainty: the pandemic increased precautionary savings.

principles.principle: Money Demand is Homogeneous of Degree 1 in Prices in the Long Run (No Money Illusion)
principles.statement: In the long run, a doubling of the price level doubles nominal money demand, leaving real money demand unchanged. This property, called long-run monetary neutrality, is a fundamental assumption of most macroeconomic models. It implies that central banks cannot permanently increase real output by printing money—only prices will adjust. However, in the short run, money illusion may exist: people may not immediately adjust their nominal balances to price changes, leading to temporary real effects of monetary policy. The speed of adjustment depends on expectations, menu costs, and information frictions.
principles.application: Central banks must distinguish between short-run and long-run effects. In the short run, an increase in the money supply can lower interest rates and boost output (if prices are sticky). In the long run, the only effect is higher prices. Money demand functions used for policy analysis should allow for short-run adjustment lags (partial adjustment models) while imposing long-run homogeneity. When forecasting inflation, central banks must account for the fact that sustained money growth will eventually lead to proportional price increases, but the lag can be 1-3 years.
principles.example: From 2008-2014, the Fed tripled the monetary base, but inflation remained below 2%. Long-run homogeneity did not seem to hold. However, by 2021-2022, inflation surged to 9% as velocity normalized and the long-run relationship reasserted itself. The lag was about 8-10 years—much longer than historical averages—due to the liquidity trap and financial crisis. This illustrates that long-run neutrality holds, but the 'long run' can be very long indeed.

image.alt: Graph: Time series of M2, price level, and real M2; M2 and P both rise after 2008; real M2 initially rises then falls back to trend; long-run neutrality shown by parallel lines.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.8: Long-run neutrality: after a monetary expansion, prices eventually adjust, leaving real money balances unchanged.

principles.principle: Financial Innovation Has Permanently Reduced Money Demand and Increased Interest Elasticity
principles.pattern: Over the past 50 years, financial innovation has consistently reduced the demand for narrow money (M1) and increased the sensitivity of money demand to interest rates. Each innovation—NOW accounts, ATMs, debit cards, online banking, mobile payments, instant payment systems—has lowered the transaction costs of moving between money and interest-bearing assets. As a result, households and firms hold smaller cash balances relative to income and respond more strongly to interest rate changes. This trend has important implications for monetary policy: the velocity of M1 has fallen secularly (making M1 an unreliable indicator), and the interest elasticity of money demand has risen (making money demand more sensitive to policy rate changes). Central banks have responded by shifting from money supply targeting to interest rate targeting, and by broadening their focus to include credit conditions and financial stability.
principles.application: Monetary policymakers should not assume that historical money demand relationships will hold in the future. Each wave of financial innovation creates a structural break. Central banks must continuously re-estimate money demand functions, include innovation proxies (e.g., number of ATMs, credit card transaction volume, mobile payment adoption), and use judgment alongside models. The decline in money demand also means that the same money supply growth will produce higher inflation today than in the past (because people hold less idle cash). However, the Fed has largely abandoned money supply as a policy guide, relying instead on interest rates and forward guidance.
principles.example: In 1980, M1 velocity was 7. By 2023, it had fallen to 1.2. A household that held 10% of income as cash in 1980 holds only 2% of income as cash today. This decline is not due to lower interest rates (rates were high in 1980, low in 2023) but to financial innovation. The same \$1 trillion increase in M1 in 1980 would have caused high inflation; in 2023, it caused little inflation because the extra money was absorbed by lower velocity (people held it as idle balances). This illustrates how financial innovation changes the transmission of monetary policy.

image.alt: Time series: M1 velocity falling from 7 (1980) to 1.2 (2023); vertical lines marking ATM introduction (1980s), debit cards (1990s), mobile payments (2010s), FedNow (2023).
image.caption: Figure 7.3.9: Secular decline in M1 velocity due to financial innovation; each wave of innovation permanently reduces money demand.

worked_examples.title: Estimating Income and Interest Elasticities from Historical Data

image.alt: Table: Year 1 (Y=10T, i=5%, M=5T); Year 2 (Y=11T, i=6%, M=5.2T); elasticity calculations shown.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.10: Worked example: calculating income and interest elasticities from two years of data.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has the following data:

• Year 1: Real GDP = \$10 trillion, Interest rate = 5%, Real money demand (M1) = \$2 trillion
• Year 2: Real GDP = \$10.5 trillion (5% increase), Interest rate = 6% (1 percentage point increase), Real money demand = \$2.04 trillion (2% increase)

Assume the money demand function is $M = A Y^{\eta_Y} e^{\eta_i i}$ (semi-log form).

a) Calculate the income elasticity of money demand ($\eta_Y$).
b) Calculate the interest semi-elasticity of money demand ($\eta_i$).
c) If GDP grows by 3% and interest rates remain constant, what is the predicted money demand growth?
d) If the central bank wants to keep money demand constant while GDP grows 3%, by how much must it raise interest rates?
e) Explain why the interest elasticity (percentage change for 1% change in i) would be much smaller than the semi-elasticity.

step_by_step.explanation:
Income elasticity = (% change in M) / (% change in Y) holding interest rates constant. But interest rates changed from Year 1 to Year 2, so we must isolate the income effect.

First, estimate the interest effect separately. We'll use the two equations to solve for both elasticities.

Let $\eta_Y$ = income elasticity, $\eta_i$ = interest semi-elasticity (change in ln M per percentage point change in i).

Year 1: $\ln(2) = \ln(A) + \eta_Y \ln(10) + \eta_i(5)$
Year 2: $\ln(2.04) = \ln(A) + \eta_Y \ln(10.5) + \eta_i(6)$

Subtract: $\ln(2.04) - \ln(2) = \eta_Y [\ln(10.5) - \ln(10)] + \eta_i(6-5)$
$\ln(1.02) = \eta_Y \ln(1.05) + \eta_i(1)$
$0.0198 = \eta_Y (0.04879) + \eta_i$

We need a second equation or an assumption. Assume that in the absence of interest rate changes, money demand would have grown exactly with income (proportional). But we can solve if we assume a value for one elasticity. Alternatively, use the fact that the income elasticity is typically around 1. Let's assume $\eta_Y = 1$ for simplicity. Then:

$0.0198 = 1(0.04879) + \eta_i$
$\eta_i = 0.0198 - 0.04879 = -0.02899$

If we instead solve without assumption, we need another data point. Given only two points, we cannot separately identify both elasticities without additional information.

step_by_step.step: Alternative approach: calculate using partial adjustment
step_by_step.explanation:
Assume that the interest semi-elasticity is known from other studies to be -0.05. Then:

$0.0198 = \eta_Y (0.04879) + (-0.05)(1)$
$0.0198 = 0.04879\eta_Y - 0.05$
$0.0698 = 0.04879\eta_Y$
$\eta_Y = 0.0698 / 0.04879 = 1.43$

This implies money demand is income-elastic (grows faster than income).

step_by_step.step: Predict money demand growth with constant interest rates
step_by_step.explanation:
If GDP grows 3% and interest rates constant, money demand growth = $\eta_Y \times 3\%$. Using $\eta_Y = 1.43$ from above:

Money demand growth = $1.43 \times 3\% = 4.29\%$

Money demand grows faster than GDP, implying falling velocity.

step_by_step.step: Calculate interest rate change to offset GDP growth
step_by_step.explanation:
To keep money demand constant (0% growth) while GDP grows 3%, the interest rate must rise to reduce money demand by exactly the income-induced increase.

Required reduction in money demand from interest rate = -4.29% (to offset the 4.29% increase from income)

Interest semi-elasticity $\eta_i = -0.05$ means a 1 percentage point rate increase reduces money demand by 5%.

Required rate increase = (4.29%) / (5% per point) = 0.858 percentage points

If the initial rate was 5%, the new rate would need to be 5.858% to keep money demand constant.

step_by_step.step: Explain difference between semi-elasticity and elasticity
step_by_step.explanation:
Interest semi-elasticity ($\eta_i$) is the percentage change in money demand for a 1 percentage point change in the interest rate (e.g., from 5% to 6%). Interest elasticity ($\epsilon_i$) is the percentage change in money demand for a 1% change in the interest rate (e.g., from 5% to 5.05%).

Since interest rates are small numbers (0.05 = 5%), a 1 percentage point change is a 20% change in the rate (from 5% to 6% is a 20% increase). Therefore, the interest elasticity is approximately $\eta_i \times i$. If $\eta_i = -0.05$ and $i = 0.05$, then $\epsilon_i = -0.05 \times 0.05 = -0.0025$ (very small). This means money demand is very inelastic with respect to the percentage change in interest rates, but more elastic with respect to absolute changes when rates are low.

This explains why the liquidity trap occurs at low rates: when i is near zero, even a small absolute change in i is a huge percentage change, but the semi-elasticity becomes very large (approaches infinity).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Income elasticity ≈ 1.43 (given interest semi-elasticity assumption). b) Interest semi-elasticity ≈ -0.05 (assumed). c) 4.29% money demand growth. d) Raise rates by 0.858 percentage points. e) Interest elasticity = η_i × i; since i is small, elasticity is much smaller than semi-elasticity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying money demand elasticities to forecast money growth, determine offsetting interest rate changes, and distinguish between semi-elasticity and elasticity.

worked_examples.title: Financial Innovation and the Secular Decline in Money Demand

image.alt: Table: Decade 1980s (ATMs introduced) → M1 velocity 7; 1990s (debit cards) → velocity 5; 2000s (online banking) → velocity 3; 2010s (mobile payments) → velocity 1.5; 2020s (FedNow) → velocity 1.2.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.11: Financial innovation timeline and the decline in M1 velocity.

worked_examples.problem:
From 1980 to 2023, M1 velocity fell from 7 to 1.2. This decline is largely attributed to financial innovation (ATMs, debit cards, online banking, mobile payments, FedNow).

a) Using the quantity equation ($MV = PY$), explain why falling velocity means that money demand (M/P) has risen relative to income (Y).
b) If income elasticity of money demand were 1 (constant velocity), what would velocity have done from 1980 to 2023 given actual GDP growth?
c) Calculate the annual average decline in velocity from 1980 to 2023 (43 years).
d) If financial innovation reduces money demand by 2% per year, how much does velocity fall annually?
e) How does the decline in money demand affect the relationship between money supply growth and inflation?

step_by_step.step: Explain the relationship between velocity and money demand
step_by_step.explanation:
The quantity equation is $MV = PY$, where $M$ is nominal money supply, $V$ is velocity, $P$ is price level, $Y$ is real GDP. Rearranged: $M/P = Y/V$.

Real money demand ($M/P$) equals real GDP divided by velocity. If velocity falls, real money demand rises relative to income. In 1980, $V=7$, so $M/P = Y/7$ (money demand = 14% of income). In 2023, $V=1.2$, so $M/P = Y/1.2$ (money demand = 83% of income). This means households and firms hold much larger real money balances relative to their income, even though nominal balances may not have increased proportionally.

Falling velocity indicates that the same amount of money supports less spending—people are holding idle cash balances.

step_by_step.step: Calculate counterfactual velocity with constant income elasticity
step_by_step.explanation:
If income elasticity of money demand were 1, then real money demand would grow exactly with real GDP, so velocity would be constant. From 1980 to 2023, real GDP grew from \$6.5T to \$22T (238% increase). If money demand grew exactly with GDP, real money demand would also have grown 238%. Then velocity would be constant. The fact that velocity fell dramatically means the income elasticity is <1 (money demand grew slower than income) OR that other factors (financial innovation) reduced money demand independently of income.

In fact, real M1 grew from \$0.3T in 1980 to \$18T in 2023 (5,900% increase)—far faster than GDP growth (238%). This huge increase in real money demand is not explained by income growth; it is explained by falling velocity (people holding more idle cash) due to low interest rates and financial innovation that made holding money less costly.

step_by_step.step: Calculate annual average decline in velocity
step_by_step.explanation:
Velocity fell from 7 to 1.2 over 43 years.

Total decline factor = 1.2 / 7 = 0.1714 (an 82.86% decline).

Annual average decline rate: $(1.2/7)^{1/43} - 1 = (0.1714)^{0.02326} - 1$
$e^{0.02326 \times \ln(0.1714)} - 1 = e^{0.02326 \times (-1.763)} - 1 = e^{-0.0410} - 1 = 0.9598 - 1 = -0.0402$ ( -4.02% per year)

Velocity fell at an average rate of 4% per year for 43 years.

step_by_step.step: Calculate velocity decline from 2% annual money demand reduction
step_by_step.explanation:
From $M/P = Y/V$, if financial innovation reduces real money demand by 2% per year independent of income, then $V$ must rise or fall? Actually, if money demand falls, velocity rises. Wait: $M/P = Y/V$ → $V = Y / (M/P)$. If money demand ($M/P$) falls, velocity rises. But we observed velocity falling, meaning money demand rose relative to income. So the financial innovation effect must be more than offset by other factors (falling interest rates, uncertainty).

Let's derive: %ΔV = %ΔY - %Δ(M/P). If financial innovation reduces money demand by 2% per year (negative %Δ(M/P) = -2%), and income grows at 3% per year, then %ΔV = 3% - (-2%) = 5% (velocity rises). This is the opposite of what happened. So the observed decline in velocity must be due to money demand increasing (positive %Δ(M/P)) due to falling interest rates and increased uncertainty, offsetting the financial innovation effect.

In reality, from 1980-2023: %ΔY ≈ 2.5% per year, %Δ(M/P) ≈ 8% per year (real M1 growth), so %ΔV = 2.5% - 8% = -5.5% per year (close to the -4% calculated). The positive money demand effect (from falling rates, liquidity trap, uncertainty) dominated the negative effect from financial innovation.

The quantity theory says %ΔM + %ΔV = %ΔP + %ΔY. If velocity is falling (negative %ΔV), then a given money supply growth will produce lower inflation (or even deflation) because the decline in velocity absorbs some of the money. This is what happened from 2008-2020: M2 grew at 6-7% annually, but velocity fell at 3-4% annually, so inflation remained low (1-2%).

Conversely, if velocity stabilizes or rises, the same money growth will produce higher inflation. This is what happened in 2021-2022: velocity stopped falling and actually rose slightly, causing inflation to surge even though M2 growth had slowed.

Financial innovation has permanently lowered the level of velocity, but the rate of change of velocity matters for inflation. Central banks must forecast both money growth and velocity changes to predict inflation. This is why the Fed no longer targets money supply—velocity is too unpredictable.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Falling velocity means real money demand has risen relative to income (M/P = Y/V). b) Velocity would have been constant. c) -4.0% per year average decline. d) A 2% reduction in money demand would raise velocity by 2% (if income constant). e) Falling velocity reduces inflationary impact of money growth; velocity stabilization can cause inflation surge.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the quantity equation to analyze the secular decline in velocity, distinguish between income elasticity and financial innovation effects, and understand implications for inflation.

process_flow.process: Changes in income (GDP) shift money demand proportionally (income effect)
process_flow.description: When real GDP increases, households and firms need more money to conduct transactions, shifting the money demand curve outward.
process_flow.mechanism: Income elasticity $\eta_Y$ determines the magnitude. In the short run, $\eta_Y$ may be less than 1 (people adjust slowly). In the long run, $\eta_Y$ is closer to 1. Central banks forecast GDP growth to predict money demand changes and adjust money supply accordingly to maintain interest rate targets.

image.alt: Graph: Money demand curve shifting right when income rises; equilibrium interest rate rises if money supply fixed.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.12: Step 1 - Income effect: higher GDP increases money demand, shifting the curve right.

process_flow.process: Changes in the price level shift nominal money demand proportionally (price level effect)
process_flow.description: When the price level rises, people need more nominal money to buy the same real goods, shifting nominal money demand outward. Real money demand is unchanged in the long run.
process_flow.mechanism: Long-run price elasticity = 1. Short-run adjustments may be incomplete due to sticky expectations or money illusion. Central banks must distinguish between real and nominal money demand when setting policy.

image.alt: Graph: Nominal money demand shifting right when price level rises; real money demand unchanged (long-run neutrality).
image.caption: Figure 7.3.13: Step 2 - Price level effect: higher prices increase nominal money demand proportionally, leaving real money demand unchanged.

process_flow.process: Changes in interest rates cause movements along the money demand curve (opportunity cost effect)
process_flow.description: When interest rates rise, the opportunity cost of holding money increases, so households and firms reduce money balances—a movement up along the demand curve.
process_flow.mechanism: The interest semi-elasticity determines the magnitude. At very low rates (near zero), the curve becomes flat (liquidity trap). At high rates, the curve is steeper (inelastic). Central banks use interest rate changes as their primary policy tool, moving along the money demand curve.

image.alt: Graph: Movement along money demand curve from point A to point B when interest rate rises; money demand falls.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.14: Step 3 - Interest rate effect: higher rates cause a movement along the money demand curve, reducing money demand.

process_flow.process: Financial innovation and uncertainty shift the money demand curve over time (structural effects)
process_flow.description: New payment technologies (ATMs, debit cards, mobile payments, FedNow) reduce money demand, shifting the curve left. Increased uncertainty (recessions, crises) increases precautionary demand, shifting the curve right.
process_flow.mechanism: These structural shifts cause instability in money demand functions. Central banks must use judgment and include proxy variables (e.g., number of ATMs, credit card volume, uncertainty indices) in their models to forecast money demand accurately.

image.alt: Graph: Money demand curve shifting left (financial innovation) and right (uncertainty); net effect depends on relative magnitudes.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.15: Step 4 - Structural effects: financial innovation shifts money demand left; uncertainty shifts it right; central banks must account for both.

process_flow.process: Central bank adjusts money supply to accommodate or offset money demand changes
process_flow.description: To maintain a stable interest rate target, the central bank must increase money supply when money demand rises (accommodate) or decrease money supply when money demand falls.
process_flow.mechanism: If the central bank wants to keep interest rates constant, it must set $M_s = M_d(Y, i^*, P, Z)$. If money demand shifts right (higher income, higher prices, higher uncertainty), the central bank must increase money supply to prevent interest rates from rising. If the central bank instead targets money supply, it allows interest rates to adjust.

image.alt: Graph: Money demand shifts right; central bank increases money supply (shift right) to keep interest rate constant (accommodation).
image.caption: Figure 7.3.16: Step 5 - Policy response: the central bank accommodates money demand shifts to maintain its interest rate target.

image.alt: Master framework: Income, price level, interest rate, financial innovation, uncertainty → money demand → central bank policy response (accommodate or offset) → equilibrium interest rate → transmission to economy.
image.caption: Figure 7.3.17: Complete framework for factors affecting money demand: from determinants to policy response.

summary: Money demand is determined by income, the price level, interest rates, and secondary factors including financial innovation and uncertainty. Higher income increases money demand (income elasticity typically 0.5-1.5). Higher prices increase nominal money demand proportionally in the long run (no money illusion) but may have short-run lags. Higher interest rates reduce money demand through the opportunity cost effect (interest semi-elasticity of -0.05 to -0.10). Financial innovation (ATMs, debit cards, mobile payments, FedNow) has permanently reduced money demand, causing a secular decline in velocity from 7 in 1980 to 1.2 in 2023. Uncertainty (recessions, crises, pandemics) increases precautionary money demand, temporarily raising velocity declines. The 'missing money' puzzle of the 1970s was caused by unmodeled financial innovation. Central banks must account for all these factors when forecasting money demand; failure to do so leads to policy errors. The secular decline in money demand has made money supply targeting unreliable, leading central banks to adopt interest rate targeting instead. Understanding these factors is essential for predicting velocity, forecasting inflation, and designing monetary policy rules.
